Build a Professional 3D Game Engine Contents Conventions Math Toolbox
← Previous N.M-1 — Previous lesson title Index All lessons Next → N.M+1 — Next lesson title
Module N — Module Name · Lesson N.M

Lesson Title

A one- or two-sentence deck that states what this lesson makes possible, in plain language, without jargon the student has not met yet.

Time
≈ 3–4 hours
Prereqs
N.M-1 — Previous lesson, Conventions
Files
src/gfx/example.hpp, src/gfx/example.cpp, CMakeLists.txt
Milestone
What is on screen, or measurably different, when you finish.

By the end of this lesson you will be able to…

  • …explain why the thing works, not just that it does.
  • …derive the formula from the geometry, without looking it up.
  • …implement it, and recognise its failure mode on sight.
  • …debug it when it goes wrong.
Contents
  1. The Problem
  2. Building Intuition
  3. The Theory
    1. The derivation
    2. A worked example, by hand
  4. Implementation
  5. Complete Code Listings
  6. Build & Run
  7. Common Pitfalls & Debugging
  8. Exercises
  9. Recap & Next
  10. Further Reading

1The Problem

Open by making the student feel the gap. Not "today we will learn X", but "here is a thing you cannot currently do, and here is what it looks like when you try." Describe the artifact precisely — the shape of the wrongness — before naming its cause.

The failure mode

Where a concept exists to prevent an artifact, show the artifact first: the overdraw chaos without a z-buffer, the swimming texture without perspective correction, the washed-out midtones without gamma. Name the symptom, then the cause, then the fix. Pedagogy §5 — this is not optional garnish.

2Building Intuition

Reason in pictures before symbols. A matrix is "where the basis vectors land." A dot product is "how much of one vector lies along another — its shadow." Perspective is similar triangles. If the intuition is right, the algebra becomes bookkeeping rather than magic.

Every diagram is referenced from the body text — as Figure 1 is here — and never dumped at the end of a section. A diagram the prose does not point at is decoration.

3The Theory

The derivation

Derive, do not decree. Build the result step by step from the picture above. If a full derivation is genuinely out of scope, say so plainly, give the intuition anyway, and name a reference that finishes the job — never leave "it just works this way" standing.

\[ \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = \|\mathbf{a}\|\,\|\mathbf{b}\|\cos\theta \] dot(a, b) = length(a) * length(b) * cos(theta)

State the equation in prose too, so the page survives a dead CDN: the dot product of two vectors equals the product of their lengths times the cosine of the angle between them. An equation must never be the only carrier of a fact.

A worked example, by hand

Worked example — real numbers, pushed through by hand

Take a = (3, 4, 0) and b = (1, 0, 0).

  • dot(a, b) = 3·1 + 4·0 + 0·0 = 3
  • |a| = sqrt(9 + 16 + 0) = 5, and |b| = 1
  • So cos θ = 3 / (5 · 1) = 0.6, giving θ ≈ 53.13°.

Read it geometrically: a's shadow on the x-axis is 3 units long, which is exactly its x-component. That is what the dot product with a unit basis vector means — it extracts a coordinate.

Every formula gets one of these

Pedagogy §4: no mathematical idea ships without at least one example with real numbers carried through by hand. Verify the arithmetic before publishing — §10 makes wrong arithmetic a correctness bug, not a typo.

C++ in place — <feature name>

The first time a C++ feature appears (RAII, ownership, move semantics, references vs pointers, templates, const-correctness, translation units), it gets a short dedicated explainer right here, at the point of use — never a forward reference to a chapter the student has not read.

⚠ Verify

Used when an API detail is not confirmed against an authoritative source. State the conceptually correct usage, then name the exact source to check — e.g. "⚠ VERIFY: field order of SDL_GPUFooInfo against SDL3/SDL_gpu.h." An honest flag beats a confident fabrication; the student is about to type this in.

4Implementation

Narrate the build-up in small steps. Each snippet is short enough to hold in your head, and each is followed by why it is shaped that way. The full files come later, in §Complete Code Listings — this section is for understanding, that one is for copying.

Explain the decision the snippet embodies. Why [[nodiscard]]? Because dropping the result is always a bug. Why noexcept? Because the engine core does not use exceptions, and saying so in the type system is free documentation the compiler enforces.

5Complete Code Listings

Every file this lesson touched, in full. Nothing elided.

6Build & Run

Then run the demo:

What you should see

Describe the expected output precisely enough that the student can tell "correct" from "nearly correct" — the second is the dangerous one. Where a picture helps, mock it with an SVG rather than prose alone.

Checkpoint

A concrete, checkable claim: "a 640×480 window titled 'Engine', filled with a horizontal red-to-green gradient, closing cleanly on Esc with no console warnings." If any part of that is off, the pitfalls below are where to look.

7Common Pitfalls & Debugging

Nothing renders — black window, no errors
Symptom
The window opens and stays black. No warnings on the console.
Cause
The real, mechanical reason — not a guess. Silence plus blackness usually means work was submitted to something that was never presented, or was culled away.
Fix
The specific change, and how to confirm it worked.
Geometry vanishes when you add culling — triangles disappear
Symptom
Everything drew fine until back-face culling was enabled; now the mesh is gone.
Cause
Winding. Your triangles are clockwise in screen space while the pipeline is set to treat counter-clockwise as front-facing, so every face is being discarded as a back face.
Fix
Reverse the vertex order, or set front_face to match. Confirm against Conventions — this course is CCW-front, cull-back, everywhere.

8Exercises

N.M.1A guided tweakwarm-up

A small, unambiguous change that proves the concept landed.

Hint

A nudge, not the answer.

Solution

Given in full when it is short enough to be worth reading.

N.M.2An engine challengeopen

Open-ended, with real design latitude — the kind of thing that has more than one defensible answer.

Solution sketch

The shape of a good answer and the tradeoff to weigh, rather than code to copy.

9Recap & Next

What the engine can do now that it could not before

  • The concrete new capability, stated as a capability.
  • The idea that will keep paying off later in the course.

Next: N.M+1 — Next lesson title. The bridge — what today leaves unfinished, and why that is exactly the next lesson's problem.

10Further Reading

  • Scratchapixel The relevant page, with a note on what it adds.
  • Real-Time Rendering Chapter N.N — the authoritative treatment.
  • SDL3 wiki The exact page for any API used here.
  • Gaffer on Games Where relevant to loops, time, or networking.
STATE — resume key 
course: Build a Professional 3D Game Engine (SDL3 + C++20)
version: 1.0

conventions:
  world: right-handed, Y-up, -Z forward
  clip: left-handed, +Y up, z in [0,1] (SDL_GPU; projection absorbs the flip)
  matrices: column vectors, v' = M*v, column-major storage
  winding: CCW = front, cull back (set explicitly on every pipeline)
  units: 1 unit = 1 metre; radians internally
  axis colours: x/y/z = red/green/blue

completed:
  - 0.1 — Lesson title

capabilities:
  - what the engine can currently do, one line each

files:
  src/: main.cpp
  src/gfx/: example.hpp, example.cpp
  docs/: index.html, conventions.html, math-toolbox.html, cpp-style.html

next: 0.2 — Next lesson title
